1. https://calendar.google.com/ - Google Calendar Help: Schedule a meeting  
2. https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/office/schedule-a-meeting-with-microsoft-outlook-5c9877bc-9c4d-4aee-8c08-5a52d6b0b67e - Microsoft Outlook: Schedule a meeting  
3. https://www.lucidmeetings.com/glossary/scheduling-meetings - Lucid Meetings: Guide to Scheduling Meetings
column7: Scheduling Meetings is valuable for cross-functional agile teams because it helps to achieve deliverables like Meeting Agendas, which help a team stay organized and focused during all Product Milestones. This activity makes sure everyone knows what will be discussed and when, so the team can make decisions, solve problems, and keep projects moving forward.
column8: The duty owner for project management collaborates closely with all other duties to ensure that meeting agendas are prepared and delivered effectively for each product milestone. By coordinating with various duties, the project management duty gathers input on discussion topics, aligns schedules, and clarifies expectations, making sure that the agendas reflect the needs and priorities of everyone involved. This collaboration helps set clear objectives for each meeting, supports decision-making, and fosters an environment where all participants feel empowered to contribute, which is essential for achieving productive outcomes and keeping the project on track.
column9:
To be great at scheduling meetings while working with other cross-functional duties, the Project Management duty needs strong skills in meeting facilitation, attention to detail, collaboration, critical thinking, decision-making, perseverance, organization, commitment, expectation setting, initiative, logical thinking, problem-solving, time management, and effective verbal and written communication. Key practices include decision-making, ownership, service leadership, psychological safety, self-organization, and empowerment. These skills and practices help the duty owner coordinate with all other duties to gather input, align schedules, clarify expectations, and prepare clear meeting agendas that reflect the needs and priorities of everyone involved.

These skills directly affect the success of delivering meeting agendas within given milestones by ensuring that meetings are purposeful, well-structured, and inclusive. When the Project Management duty uses these skills, meetings are more likely to stay focused on project goals, support effective decision-making, and foster an environment where all participants feel valued and motivated to contribute. This leads to productive outcomes, smooth progress, and effective communication throughout the project, helping to keep deliverables on track and aligned with each product milestone.
column10:
Here's how the Project Management duty carries out the activity Scheduling Meetings with others  
Step 1  
The Project Management duty collaborates with all other duties to gather input on discussion topics, align schedules, and clarify expectations for upcoming meetings related to project milestones.  

Step 2  
Using this input, the Project Management duty prepares clear and detailed meeting agendas that reflect the needs and priorities of everyone involved, ensuring that each agenda sets clear objectives and supports productive discussions.  

Step 3  
The Project Management duty facilitates the meeting by guiding the discussion according to the agenda, encouraging participation, supporting decision-making, and fostering an environment where all duties feel empowered to contribute, which helps keep the project on track and ensures effective communication.
column11:
For scheduling meetings, the project management duty is both responsible for organizing and coordinating the meetings and accountable for ensuring their success. All duties are consulted, meaning they provide advice and feedback on the meeting topics and agendas. After the meetings are scheduled and agendas are prepared, all duties—including business analysis, development, graphic design, information security analysis, product management, product operations, product ownership, project management, QA analysis, scrum master, solutions architecture, UX design, UX research, UX strategy, UX writing, account management, and agile coaching—are informed about the work.

These duties work together by having project management coordinate with everyone to gather input, align schedules, and clarify expectations, ensuring that meeting agendas reflect the needs and priorities of all involved. This collaborative approach supports clear communication, effective decision-making, and a sense of ownership, which helps meetings run smoothly and keeps the project moving forward.

column5: SEO Design involves creating strategies to improve a website’s visibility on search engines by using skills like attention to detail, critical thinking, problem-solving, and effective communication. Duties include researching keywords, optimizing content, and organizing site structure to align with search engine algorithms. Practices such as decision-making, ownership, and empowerment ensure consistent improvement and adaptation to changes. These skills and practices enhance UX Design by making websites easier to find, navigate, and understand, which leads to a better overall experience for users and higher engagement.
column6:
Here are some resources to learn about how to perform the activity:  
1. Moz Beginner's Guide to SEO – https://moz.com/beginners-guide-to-seo  
2. Google Search Central SEO Starter Guide – https://developers.google.com/search/docs/fundamentals/seo-starter-guide  
3. HubSpot SEO Training Course – https://academy.hubspot.com/courses/seo
column7: Search Engine Optimization (SEO) Design is valuable for cross-functional agile teams because it helps achieve deliverables like an SEO Strategy, which guides how a product or service can be found more easily online during milestones such as Experience Design. This activity makes sure that when people search for something, they can find your website or app, and it helps the team create a plan to make the website show up higher in search results. The main milestone this activity supports is Experience Design, and the main deliverable it helps produce is the SEO Strategy.
column8: The UX Design duty works closely with other duties such as Business Analysis, Product Management, Product Ownership, UX Research, UX Strategy, and UX Writing to deliver the SEO Strategy within the Experience Design milestone. UX Design collaborates with Business Analysis to understand user needs and business goals, and with Product Management and Product Ownership to ensure the SEO Strategy aligns with the overall product direction. UX Research provides insights into user behavior and search patterns, while UX Strategy helps set the vision for how SEO supports the user experience. UX Writing ensures that content is clear, relevant, and optimized for both users and search engines. By working together, these duties organize and optimize website content and structure, select effective keywords, and make data-driven decisions to improve visibility and user engagement, resulting in a successful SEO Strategy as part of the Experience Design milestone.
column9:
To be great at Search Engine Optimization (SEO) Design while working with other cross-functional duties, the UX Design duty needs strong attention to detail, critical thinking, and problem-solving skills to organize content, select effective keywords, and optimize site structure. Collaboration, verbal and written communication, and logical thinking are essential for working closely with Business Analysis, Product Management, Product Ownership, UX Research, UX Strategy, and UX Writing. Practices like decision-making, ownership, and empowerment help the UX Design duty take initiative, adapt strategies based on data, and ensure that SEO efforts align with user needs and business goals.

These skills and practices directly affect the success of delivering the SEO Strategy within the Experience Design milestone. Effective collaboration and communication help align all duties toward a common goal, while strong organization and time management ensure that deliverables are completed on schedule. Critical thinking and data-driven decision-making allow the UX Design duty to quickly adjust strategies for better results, leading to improved website visibility, higher user engagement, and a successful SEO Strategy that meets both user and business needs.
column10:
Here's how the UX (User Experience) Design duty carries out the activity Search Engine Optimization (SEO) Design with others  
Step 1  
UX Design collaborates with Business Analysis, Product Management, and Product Ownership duties to understand user needs, business goals, and product direction, ensuring the SEO Strategy supports both user experience and organizational objectives.

Step 2  
UX Design works with UX Research and UX Strategy duties to gather insights on user behavior and search patterns, then organizes and optimizes website content and structure, selects effective keywords, and aligns the SEO Strategy with the overall vision for user experience.

Step 3  
UX Design partners with UX Writing to create clear, relevant, and search-optimized content, and uses data-driven decision-making to refine the SEO Strategy, improving website visibility, navigation, and user engagement as part of the Experience Design milestone.
column11:
For Search Engine Optimization (SEO) Design, the UX (User Experience) Strategy duty is responsible for performing the activity, using skills like attention to detail, problem-solving, and collaboration to create and implement the SEO strategy. The UX (User Experience) Design duty is accountable, overseeing the success of the SEO Design and ensuring it aligns with the overall user experience goals. Duties such as UX Research, UX Writing, Product Ownership, Product Management, Scrum Master, Development, Graphic Design, Solutions Architecture, and Information Security Analysis are consulted, providing advice and feedback throughout the process. All duties, including Business Analysis, Product Management, Product Ownership, UX Research, UX Strategy, UX Writing, and others, are informed about the work after it is completed.

These duties work together by sharing expertise and feedback, ensuring the SEO strategy is well-informed, effective, and aligned with both user needs and business objectives. The responsible duty organizes and executes the SEO work, the accountable duty ensures quality and alignment, consulted duties offer valuable insights, and informed duties stay updated on progress. This collaboration leads to a well-optimized website that is easy to find and use, resulting in higher user engagement and satisfaction.

column5: Secondary Research in UX involves gathering and analyzing existing information from various sources to understand user needs and behaviors. This duty requires skills such as critical thinking, attention to detail, organization, and written communication to synthesize data effectively into deliverables like research reports, affinity maps, empathy maps, and user journey maps. Practices like agility, ownership, and continuous growth ensure the research adapts to new insights and maintains psychological safety and empowerment throughout the process. These skills and practices enhance the success of Secondary Research by enabling thorough, accurate analysis and clear communication, which inform better decision-making and improve the overall user experience.
column6:
Here are some resources to learn about how to perform the activity:  
1. https://libguides.mit.edu/c.php?g=175963&p=1159480 (MIT Libraries: Secondary Research Guide)  
2. https://www.scribbr.com/methodology/secondary-research/ (Scribbr: Secondary Research—Definition, Methods & Examples)  
3. https://www.snhu.edu/about-us/newsroom/2022/03/secondary-research (Southern New Hampshire University: What is Secondary Research?)
column7: Secondary Research is valuable for teams because it helps them gather and understand information about users, competitors, and the market, which is important for making smart decisions. This activity helps teams create deliverables like a Research Analysis, Research Report, Research Plan, Affinity Map, Empathy Map, and User Journey Map, which all help the team see what users need and how they behave. Secondary Research supports milestones like Continuous Discovery and Acceptance Testing, making sure the team is always learning and improving their product or service.
column8: The duty owner of UX Research collaborates closely with other duties such as Business Analysis, Product Management, Product Ownership, UX Design, and UX Strategy to deliver key research-based deliverables throughout project milestones like Continuous Discovery and Acceptance Testing. For example, while the UX Research duty gathers and analyzes existing information to create research analyses and affinity maps, the Business Analysis duty helps interpret this data in the context of business goals, and Product Management and Product Ownership duties use these insights to shape product direction and prioritize features. UX Design and UX Strategy duties then apply the synthesized findings—such as empathy maps and user journey maps—to inform design decisions and strategic planning. By working together, these duties ensure that research deliverables are actionable, relevant, and directly support the ongoing improvement of the user experience at each milestone.
column9:
To be great at Secondary Research while working with other cross-functional duties, the UX Research duty needs strong critical thinking to evaluate the quality and relevance of existing data, attention to detail to accurately extract and organize insights, and excellent organization to manage large volumes of information. Practices like agility and continuous growth help the duty adapt to new findings and improve research methods, while ownership and initiative ensure a thorough and proactive approach. Effective collaboration, verbal and written communication, and logical thinking are also essential for sharing findings and aligning with duties such as Business Analysis, Product Management, Product Ownership, UX Design, and UX Strategy.

These skills and practices directly impact the success of delivering research analysis, reports, plans, and affinity maps within project milestones like Continuous Discovery and Acceptance Testing. By applying critical thinking and organization, the UX Research duty provides clear, actionable insights that other duties can use to make informed decisions and prioritize features. Agility and continuous growth ensure that research remains relevant and up-to-date, supporting a user-centered approach and enabling the delivery of high-quality, timely deliverables that drive the ongoing improvement of the user experience.
column10:
Here's how the UX (User Experience) Research duty carries out the activity Secondary Research with others  
Step 1  
The UX Research duty gathers and reviews existing information from sources like market reports, academic articles, and competitor analysis, using critical thinking and attention to detail to ensure the data is relevant and reliable.  

Step 2  
The UX Research duty collaborates with Business Analysis, Product Management, and Product Ownership duties to interpret the collected data in the context of business goals and product direction, organizing findings into research analyses and affinity maps.  

Step 3  
The UX Research duty works with UX Design and UX Strategy duties to synthesize insights into deliverables such as empathy maps and user journey maps, ensuring that the research findings directly inform design decisions and strategic planning throughout project milestones like Continuous Discovery and Acceptance Testing.
column11:
For Secondary Research, the UX Research duty is both responsible for performing the research and accountable for overseeing its success. Duties such as Product Ownership, Product Management, UX Strategy, UX Design, Business Analysis, and UX Writing are consulted, providing advice and feedback to ensure the research is relevant and actionable. All duties—including Business Analysis, Development, Graphic Design, Information Security Analysis, Product Management, Product Operations, Product Ownership, Project Management, QA Analysis, Scrum Master, Solutions Architecture, UX Design, UX Research, UX Strategy, UX Writing, Account Management, and Agile Coaching—are informed about the research outcomes after completion.

These duties work together by having UX Research gather and analyze information, while consulted duties contribute their expertise to shape the research direction and interpretation. Once the research is complete, all informed duties receive the findings, enabling them to make better decisions and align their work with user needs and business goals. This collaboration ensures that research deliverables like analysis reports and affinity maps are accurate, useful, and drive continuous improvement in the user experience.

column5: The Security Protocol Definition involves creating a clear and detailed network architecture that ensures the protection of information systems. This duty requires skills such as attention to detail, critical thinking, decision-making, and problem-solving to identify potential vulnerabilities and design effective security measures. Practices like ownership, service leadership, and empowerment foster a responsible and proactive approach, while psychological safety encourages open communication and collaboration. Strong organization, time management, and communication skills support the thorough documentation and explanation of protocols. These combined skills and practices are essential for successfully defining security protocols that safeguard data and maintain system integrity.
column6:
Here are some resources to learn about how to perform the activity:  
1. NIST Special Publication 800-12: An Introduction to Information Security (https://csrc.nist.gov/publications/detail/sp/800-12/rev-1/final)  
2. OWASP Security Principles (https://owasp.org/www-project-top-ten/2017/A10_2017-Insufficient_Logging%26Monitoring.html)  
3. SANS Institute: Security Policy Templates and Guidelines (https://www.sans.org/information-security-policy/)
column7: Security Protocol Definition is valuable for cross-functional agile teams because it helps to achieve deliverables like Network Architecture, which are important for keeping information safe and organized during milestones such as Architecture. This activity makes sure that everyone knows how to protect data and set up secure systems, so the team can build strong and safe products or services.
column8: The duty of Information Security Analysis works closely with other duties such as Business Analysis, Development, Product Management, Product Ownership, and Solutions Architecture to define security protocols that protect the network architecture. Information Security Analysis gathers input from Business Analysis to understand business needs and risks, collaborates with Development to ensure protocols are technically feasible, and coordinates with Product Management and Product Ownership to align security measures with product goals. By working with Solutions Architecture, Information Security Analysis ensures that the security protocols fit seamlessly into the overall network design. This collaboration ensures that the deliverable—Network Architecture—meets security requirements and supports the achievement of the Architecture milestone.
column9:
To be great at Security Protocol Definition while working with other cross-functional duties, Information Security Analysis needs strong attention to detail, critical thinking, and decision-making skills to design thorough and effective security rules. Collaboration, verbal and written communication, and logical thinking are essential for gathering input from Business Analysis, Development, Product Management, Product Ownership, and Solutions Architecture. Practices like ownership, empowerment, and psychological safety help Information Security Analysis take responsibility for outcomes and encourage open sharing of ideas and concerns, making protocols more practical and adaptable.

These skills and practices directly affect the success of delivering the Network Architecture within the Architecture milestone. By using strong organization, time management, and problem-solving, Information Security Analysis ensures that security protocols are clearly defined, align with business and technical needs, and are integrated smoothly into the overall network design. This cross-functional approach helps prevent delays, reduces risks, and ensures that the deliverables meet security requirements, supporting the timely achievement of project milestones.
column10:
Here's how the Information Security Analysis duty carries out the activity Security Protocol Definition with others  
Step 1  
Information Security Analysis gathers input from Business Analysis to understand business needs, risks, and requirements, ensuring that the security protocols address real-world concerns and align with organizational goals.

Step 2  
Information Security Analysis collaborates with Development to review technical feasibility and works with Product Management and Product Ownership to make sure the protocols support product objectives. This duty also coordinates with Solutions Architecture to integrate the protocols into the overall network design.

Step 3  
Information Security Analysis documents the security protocols clearly, communicates them to all involved duties, and encourages open feedback to refine and adapt the protocols as needed. This ensures the deliverable—Network Architecture—meets security requirements and supports the achievement of the Architecture milestone.
column11:
For Security Protocol Definition, the duty of Information Security Analysis is both responsible for performing the activity and accountable for overseeing its success. Product Ownership, Business Analysis, Development, Product Management, and QA Analysis are consulted, providing advice and feedback to ensure the protocols are practical, align with business needs, and meet technical requirements. All duties, including Business Analysis, Development, Graphic Design, Information Security Analysis, Product Management, Product Operations, Product Ownership, Project Management, QA Analysis, Scrum Master, Solutions Architecture, UX Design, UX Research, UX Strategy, UX Writing, Account Management, and Agile Coaching, are informed about the work after it is completed.

These duties work together by having Information Security Analysis lead the definition and documentation of security protocols, while gathering input from consulted duties to address business, technical, and quality considerations. Once the protocols are defined, all relevant duties are kept informed to ensure alignment and awareness across the project. This collaborative approach ensures that the network architecture is secure, meets project milestones, and supports the overall goals of the organization.

column5: Server-side coding involves writing and maintaining the code that runs on a server to handle requests, process data, and deliver responses to users. This duty requires skills such as attention to detail, algorithm design, knowledge of server-side scripting and object-oriented programming languages, database queries, and command line scripting. Effective collaboration, critical thinking, decision-making, perseverance, organization, and communication are essential practices that support ownership, empowerment, and psychological safety during development. Deliverables typically include functional code and API libraries that enable interaction between different software components. Mastery of these skills and practices ensures reliable, efficient, and secure server-side functionality, which is crucial for the success of the development milestones and the overall project.
column6:
Here are some resources to learn about how to perform the activity:  
1. Mozilla Developer Network (MDN) – Server-side website programming: https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Learn/Server-side  
2. freeCodeCamp – Back End Development and APIs: https://www.freecodecamp.org/learn/back-end-development-and-apis/  
3. W3Schools – Server Side Scripting: https://www.w3schools.com/whatis/whatis_server.asp
column7: Server-Side Coding is valuable for cross-functional agile teams because it helps to achieve deliverables like Code and API Library, which are important for building the main parts of a website or app during milestones such as Development and Architecture, and also during Development again to make sure everything works smoothly. This means the duty is about writing the instructions that make things happen behind the scenes, like when you log in to a website or use an app, and making sure other parts of the project can use these instructions easily.
column8:
The development duty works closely with business analysis, product ownership, QA analysis, solutions architecture, and UX design duties to deliver high-quality code and API libraries within project milestones. Business analysis and product ownership duties clarify requirements and priorities, which guide the development duty in designing and implementing server-side solutions. Solutions architecture duty provides technical direction and ensures the code aligns with the overall system structure, while UX design duty offers insights to make sure the server-side logic supports a seamless user experience.

Throughout the development and architecture milestones, the development duty collaborates with QA analysis duty to test and refine the code and APIs, ensuring reliability and performance. By maintaining open communication and sharing progress, the development duty integrates feedback from all involved duties, resulting in deliverables that meet project goals and user needs.
column9:
To be great at server-side coding while working with other cross-functional duties, the development duty needs strong skills in attention to detail, algorithm design, server-side scripting, object-oriented programming, database queries, and API development. Effective practices such as decision-making, ownership, self-organization, and empowerment help the development duty take initiative and solve problems creatively. Collaboration, communication, and time management are essential for integrating code and APIs with input from business analysis, product ownership, QA analysis, solutions architecture, and UX design duties. Perseverance and critical thinking enable the development duty to overcome technical challenges and deliver reliable, efficient, and scalable solutions.

These skills and practices directly impact the ability to deliver code and API libraries within project milestones. By maintaining open communication and sharing progress with other duties, the development duty ensures that requirements are clear, technical direction is followed, and user experience is supported. Collaboration with QA analysis helps refine and test deliverables, leading to high-quality, reliable outcomes. Strong organization and time management allow the development duty to meet deadlines, while critical thinking and problem-solving ensure that deliverables meet project goals and user needs.
column10:
Here's how the Development duty carries out the activity Server-Side Coding with others  
Step 1  
The Development duty collaborates with Business Analysis and Product Ownership duties to clarify requirements and priorities, using this information to design server-side solutions that align with project goals. The Development duty also works with Solutions Architecture duty to ensure the code structure fits the overall system and with UX Design duty to make sure the server-side logic supports a smooth user experience.

Step 2  
The Development duty writes and maintains server-side code and APIs, applying skills like algorithm design, database queries, and object-oriented programming. Throughout this process, the Development duty communicates progress and integrates feedback from other duties, especially QA Analysis, to test and refine the code for reliability and performance.

Step 3  
The Development duty finalizes and delivers the code and API library, ensuring all requirements are met and the deliverables are reliable, efficient, and scalable. By maintaining open communication and sharing updates with all involved duties, the Development duty ensures the server-side code supports project milestones and user needs.
column11:
For server-side coding, the development duty is both responsible for performing the coding and accountable for the success of the activity. Duties such as business analysis, product management, UX design, UX research, UX writing, solutions architecture, information security analysis, QA analysis, and project management are consulted to provide advice and feedback throughout the process. All duties, including business analysis, development, graphic design, information security analysis, product management, product operations, product ownership, project management, QA analysis, scrum master, solutions architecture, UX design, UX research, UX strategy, UX writing, account management, and agile coaching, are informed about the work after it is completed.

These duties work together by having the development duty take ownership of writing and maintaining server-side code, while regularly seeking input from consulted duties to ensure the code meets requirements, aligns with system architecture, and supports a seamless user experience. Consulted duties help clarify needs, provide technical direction, and offer feedback, while informed duties stay updated on progress and outcomes. This collaboration ensures that the deliverables—reliable and efficient code and API libraries—are produced on time and meet project goals, with everyone understanding their part in the process.

column5: Site Map Testing involves carefully evaluating the structure and organization of a website’s content to ensure users can easily find information. This duty requires skills like attention to detail, critical thinking, and information architecture to create a clear and logical hierarchy of pages. Practices such as decision-making, ownership, and empowerment help maintain focus and confidence throughout the testing process. Effective collaboration and communication, both verbal and written, support gathering feedback and refining the sitemap. Deliverables include the sitemap itself, a research report, and analysis that highlight usability issues and improvements. These skills and practices directly impact the success of the activity by enhancing the user experience, making navigation intuitive and efficient, which ultimately leads to higher user satisfaction and engagement.
column6:
Here are some resources to learn about how to perform the activity:  
1. Nielsen Norman Group – "Card Sorting: How to Do It" (covers site map testing and information architecture): https://www.nngroup.com/articles/card-sorting/  
2. Optimal Workshop – "Tree Testing: A Beginner’s Guide": https://help.optimalworkshop.com/hc/en-us/articles/360000710716-Tree-testing-a-beginner-s-guide  
3. UX Booth – "How to Test Your Site Map": https://www.uxbooth.com/articles/how-to-test-your-site-map/
column7:
Site Map Testing is valuable for cross-functional agile teams because it helps to achieve deliverables like a Sitemap, a Research Report, and Research Analysis, which help a team organize and understand how people find information on a website during milestones such as Experience Design, Requirements, Continuous Discovery, and Acceptance Testing. 

In simple terms, Site Map Testing is a duty that checks if a website’s map makes sense and is easy to use, so everyone can find what they need. This helps the team make reports and research that show what works and what doesn’t, and these are used at different steps like planning, designing, and making sure everything is right before the website is finished.
column8: The duty owner for UX Research collaborates closely with other duties such as Business Analysis, Product Management, Product Ownership, UX Design, and UX Strategy to deliver key outputs like the sitemap, research report, and research analysis. During milestones like Experience Design and Requirements, UX Research gathers user insights and feedback, which Business Analysis uses to align the site structure with business needs, while Product Management and Product Ownership ensure the deliverables meet project goals and priorities. UX Design and UX Strategy duties use the findings to refine the site’s organization and navigation, ensuring the sitemap supports both user needs and strategic objectives. This ongoing collaboration throughout milestones like Continuous Discovery and Acceptance Testing ensures that the deliverables are accurate, actionable, and contribute to a user-friendly website.
column9:
To be great at Site Map Testing, the UX Research duty needs strong attention to detail, critical thinking, and a deep understanding of information architecture and hierarchy. Effective verbal and written communication, logical thinking, and problem-solving are essential for gathering user feedback, analyzing site structure, and sharing findings with other duties. Practices like decision-making, ownership, and psychological safety help create an environment where honest feedback and thorough testing are encouraged, while collaboration and service leadership ensure that insights are shared and acted upon across all cross-functional duties.

These skills and practices directly impact the ability to deliver accurate sitemaps, research reports, and analyses within project milestones. By working closely with Business Analysis, Product Management, Product Ownership, UX Design, and UX Strategy, the UX Research duty ensures that user insights are integrated into the site’s structure and navigation. This collaboration helps align the deliverables with both user needs and business goals, making it possible to meet deadlines and produce a website that is intuitive, accessible, and effective for its users.
column10:
Here's how the UX (User Experience) Research duty carries out the activity Site Map Testing with others  
Step 1  
UX Research gathers user insights by conducting interviews, surveys, or usability tests to understand how users expect to find information on the website. This research is shared with Business Analysis and Product Management duties to ensure the sitemap aligns with both user needs and business goals.

Step 2  
UX Research collaborates with UX Design and UX Strategy duties to create and refine the sitemap, using feedback and research findings to organize content logically. During this process, Product Ownership ensures that the sitemap supports project priorities and requirements.

Step 3  
UX Research analyzes the results of site map testing, produces a research report, and shares actionable recommendations with all involved duties. This ongoing collaboration continues through milestones like Continuous Discovery and Acceptance Testing, ensuring the sitemap is intuitive, supports user navigation, and meets strategic objectives.
column11:
For Site Map Testing, the UX (User Experience) Research duty is both responsible for performing the activity and accountable for its success, ensuring the website’s structure is user-friendly and effective. Duties such as Product Ownership, Product Management, UX Strategy, UX Design, Business Analysis, and UX Writing are consulted, providing valuable advice and feedback throughout the process. All duties, including those in Business Analysis, Development, Graphic Design, Information Security Analysis, and others, are informed about the outcomes after the work is completed.

These duties work together by having UX Research lead the testing and analysis, while consulted duties contribute their expertise to refine the sitemap and align it with user and business needs. Informed duties stay updated on progress and results, supporting transparency and shared understanding. This collaboration ensures the sitemap is clear, logical, and supports a positive user experience, with each duty playing a specific part in achieving a well-organized and accessible website.

column5: Sprint Demo Facilitation involves guiding the presentation of completed work to stakeholders, ensuring clear communication and engagement through strong meeting facilitation and public speaking skills. The duty requires attention to detail, organization, and time management to prepare and deliver the Sprint Demo Presentation effectively. Practices such as decision-making, ownership, service leadership, psychological safety, and empowerment foster an environment where participants feel valued and motivated to contribute. Agile UX knowledge and collaboration enhance the demonstration’s relevance and clarity. Critical thinking, problem-solving, and logical thinking help address questions and feedback during the demo. These skills and practices directly impact the success of the activity by creating a structured, inclusive, and transparent forum that highlights progress, builds trust, and supports continuous improvement.
column6:
Here are some resources to learn about how to perform the activity:  
1. Scrum.org – How to Facilitate a Sprint Review: https://www.scrum.org/resources/blog/how-facilitate-sprint-review

3. Mountain Goat Software – Tips for Effective Sprint Demos: https://www.mountaingoatsoftware.com/blog/sprint-demo-tips
column7: Sprint Demo Facilitation is valuable for cross-functional agile teams because it helps achieve deliverables like the Sprint Demo Presentation, which shows what the team has built and learned during a sprint, and supports all Product Milestones by making sure everyone understands the progress and results. This activity lets the team share their work, get feedback, and celebrate what they’ve finished, which helps everyone stay on track and improve together.
column8: The duty owner, Scrum Master, collaborates with all other duties to ensure the Sprint Demo Presentation is prepared and delivered effectively within each product milestone. The Scrum Master’s facilitation duty relies on the completion of work by development and testing duties, the organization of requirements by product management duties, and the communication of progress by reporting duties. By coordinating these interconnected duties, the Scrum Master ensures that the deliverable—the Sprint Demo Presentation—accurately reflects the work accomplished, addresses stakeholder questions, and incorporates feedback for continuous improvement. This collaboration supports the achievement of project milestones and the overall success of the agile process.
column9:
To be great at Sprint Demo Facilitation while working with other cross-functional duties, the Scrum Master needs strong meeting facilitation, public speaking, and presentation skills to guide clear and engaging presentations. Attention to detail, organization, and time management are essential for preparing and delivering the Sprint Demo Presentation. The Scrum Master must also practice decision-making, ownership, service leadership, psychological safety, and empowerment to create an open environment where feedback is encouraged and everyone feels comfortable sharing. Collaboration, critical thinking, and effective communication with all other duties—such as development, testing, product management, and reporting—are key to ensuring the presentation accurately reflects the work completed and addresses stakeholder questions.

These skills and practices directly impact the success of delivering the Sprint Demo Presentation within given milestones. By coordinating with all other duties and fostering a collaborative, safe environment, the Scrum Master ensures that the deliverable is well-prepared, relevant, and incorporates feedback for continuous improvement. This approach helps manage stakeholder expectations, supports the achievement of project milestones, and drives the overall success of the agile process by keeping everyone aligned and focused on delivering value.
column10:
Here's how the Scrum Master duty carries out the activity Sprint Demo Facilitation with others  
Step 1  
Coordinate with development, testing, product management, and reporting duties to gather completed work, organize requirements, and collect progress updates needed for the Sprint Demo Presentation.  

Step 2  
Prepare the Sprint Demo Presentation by ensuring all deliverables are clearly documented, the agenda is set, and stakeholders’ questions are anticipated, using strong organization, attention to detail, and communication skills.  

Step 3  
Facilitate the Sprint Demo by guiding the presentation, encouraging open feedback, addressing stakeholder questions, and fostering a collaborative environment that supports continuous improvement and achievement of project milestones.

These duties work together by relying on the Scrum Master to coordinate and present the work, while the consulted duties offer insights and suggestions to improve the process. The informed duties stay updated on the results, ensuring transparency and alignment across the project. column5: Sprint Planning involves defining clear Sprint Goals and creating a detailed Sprint Plan by facilitating effective meetings, negotiating priorities, and ensuring attention to detail. The duty requires skills such as collaboration, critical thinking, decision-making, organization, and communication to align expectations and foster psychological safety and empowerment. Practices like ownership, service leadership, and agility support continuous growth and help manage stakeholder needs. These skills and practices enable the duty to guide the group in prioritizing work, setting realistic commitments, and maintaining focus, which are essential for the success of the Sprint Planning activity.
column6:
Here are some resources to learn about how to perform the activity:  
1. https://www.scrum.org/resources/what-is-sprint-planning  
2. https://www.atlassian.com/agile/scrum/sprint-planning  
3. https://www.mountaingoatsoftware.com/agile/scrum/sprint-planning-meeting
column7: Sprint Planning is valuable for cross-functional agile teams because it helps achieve deliverables like Sprint Goals and a Sprint Plan, which guide the team’s work during important milestones such as All Product Milestones and Intake. This activity makes sure everyone knows what needs to be done and when, so the team can stay organized and focused as they build and improve products or services. The Scrum Master is responsible for this duty, making sure the team sets clear goals and plans for each sprint, which helps everyone work together smoothly and reach their milestones.
column8: The duty owner, Scrum Master, collaborates closely with all other duties to ensure that Sprint Planning runs smoothly and achieves its objectives. By facilitating discussions, the Scrum Master helps other duties clarify priorities, negotiate what can be accomplished, and organize tasks in a way that aligns with upcoming product milestones and intake requirements. Through effective communication, negotiation, and organization, the Scrum Master supports other duties in setting clear Sprint Goals and creating a detailed Sprint Plan. This collaboration ensures that everyone is committed to the plan, understands their responsibilities, and is empowered to contribute, which leads to the successful delivery of the agreed-upon deliverables within the set milestones.
column9:
To be great at Sprint Planning while working with other cross-functional duties, the Scrum Master needs strong skills in meeting facilitation, negotiation, attention to detail, collaboration, critical thinking, decision-making, organization, and communication. The Scrum Master practices ownership, service leadership, psychological safety, empowerment, agility, and continuous growth to create an environment where all duties feel safe to contribute and take responsibility. By facilitating discussions, clarifying priorities, and organizing tasks, the Scrum Master helps all duties align on clear Sprint Goals and a detailed Sprint Plan that matches product milestones and intake requirements.

These skills and practices directly affect the success of delivering the deliverables within given milestones by ensuring that everyone is committed to the plan, understands their responsibilities, and is empowered to contribute. Effective negotiation and organization help set realistic goals and expectations, while clear communication and collaboration prevent misunderstandings and delays. This focused and adaptive planning process enables the group to deliver Sprint Goals and the Sprint Plan on time, supporting the achievement of all product milestones.
column10:
Here's how the Scrum Master duty carries out the activity Sprint Planning with others  
Step 1  
The Scrum Master starts by bringing together all duties to discuss the goals for the upcoming sprint, making sure everyone feels safe to share ideas and concerns. They use strong meeting facilitation and communication skills to help each duty clarify what is most important and what can realistically be accomplished, while encouraging ownership and empowerment.

Step 2  
Next, the Scrum Master guides the group in negotiating priorities and organizing tasks, using critical thinking and problem-solving to align the plan with product milestones and intake requirements. They help each duty understand their responsibilities, set clear expectations, and ensure that everyone is committed to the Sprint Goals.

Step 3  
Finally, the Scrum Master documents the agreed Sprint Goals and detailed Sprint Plan, making sure all duties are informed and empowered to contribute. They continue to support collaboration and psychological safety, so everyone stays focused and adaptive throughout the sprint, leading to successful delivery of the planned work.
column11:
The Scrum Master and Product Ownership duties are responsible for performing Sprint Planning, with the Scrum Master also being accountable for overseeing its success. Account Management, Product Management, and Business Analysis duties are consulted to provide advice and feedback during the planning process. After Sprint Planning is completed, all duties—including Business Analysis, Development, Graphic Design, Information Security Analysis, Product Management, Product Operations, Product Ownership, Project Management, QA Analysis, Scrum Master, Solutions Architecture, UX (User Experience) Design, UX (User Experience) Research, UX (User Experience) Strategy, UX (User Experience) Writing, Account Management, and Agile Coaching—are informed about the outcomes.

These duties work together by having the Scrum Master facilitate discussions and ensure the planning process is organized and effective, while Product Ownership helps define priorities and goals. Consulted duties contribute their expertise and feedback, making the plan more robust and realistic. Once the plan is set, all informed duties are kept up to date, ensuring everyone understands the Sprint Goals and Sprint Plan. This clear division of responsibility, accountability, consultation, and information sharing enables focused collaboration, shared commitment, and successful delivery of the Sprint’s objectives.

column5: Sprint Retrospective Facilitation involves guiding a reflective meeting where participants review the completed sprint to identify successes, challenges, and areas for improvement. The duty requires skills such as meeting facilitation, conflict resolution, delivering and receiving feedback, critical thinking, and time management to ensure productive discussions and actionable outcomes. Practices like psychological safety, empowerment, service leadership, and ownership foster an open environment where honest communication and continuous growth are encouraged. The facilitator’s ability to build influence, generate buy-in, and apply business acumen helps align the retrospective with broader project goals. Deliverables include clear Sprint Retro Action Items and Agile Maturity Measurements that drive improvement in future sprints. Mastery of these skills and practices directly impacts the success of the activity by creating a safe space for candid dialogue, ensuring focused decision-making, and maintaining commitment to agreed actions, ultimately enhancing the agility and effectiveness of the process.
column6:
Here are some resources to learn about how to perform the activity:  
1. https://www.scrum.org/resources/what-is-a-sprint-retrospective  
2. https://www.atlassian.com/agile/scrum/retrospectives  
3. https://www.mountaingoatsoftware.com/agile/scrum/sprint-retrospective
column7: Sprint Retrospective Facilitation is valuable for teams because it helps them reach all product milestones by creating important deliverables like Sprint Retro Action Items and Agile Maturity Measurements. These deliverables help teams see what went well, what could be better, and how they can improve together in the future. By looking back at how they worked during a sprint, teams can make changes that help them get better at building products and services, making sure they keep getting closer to their goals every time.
column8:
The duty owner, the Scrum Master, collaborates closely with all other duties to ensure that the Sprint Retrospective Facilitation activity is effective and productive. By using skills like meeting facilitation and conflict resolution, the Scrum Master encourages open communication among the duties, helping them share honest feedback and insights about the completed sprint. This collaboration allows the Scrum Master to capture meaningful Sprint Retro Action Items and Agile Maturity Measurements, which are essential deliverables for continuous improvement.

Throughout all product milestones, the Scrum Master’s duty works with others by fostering a safe environment where every duty feels empowered to contribute ideas and discuss challenges. By practicing service leadership and promoting self-organization, the Scrum Master helps all duties take ownership of their actions and growth. This joint effort ensures that the deliverables from the retrospective are relevant and actionable, supporting ongoing progress and the achievement of project milestones.
column9:
To be great at Sprint Retrospective Facilitation while working with other cross-functional duties, the Scrum Master needs strong skills in meeting facilitation, conflict resolution, delivering and receiving feedback, collaboration, and time management. Practicing service leadership, ownership, self-organization, and continuous growth helps create a psychologically safe environment where all duties feel empowered to share honest feedback and ideas. Attention to detail and critical thinking are essential for capturing meaningful action items and measuring agile maturity, while advocacy and building influence help generate buy-in for positive change.

These skills and practices directly affect the success of delivering Sprint Retro Action Items and Agile Maturity Measurements within given milestones. By fostering open communication and empowerment, the Scrum Master ensures that discussions are productive and decisions are clear, leading to relevant and actionable deliverables. This collaborative approach supports ongoing progress, helps all duties take ownership of improvements, and increases the likelihood of meeting project milestones efficiently.
column10:
Here's how the Scrum Master duty carries out the activity Sprint Retrospective Facilitation with others  
Step 1  
The Scrum Master prepares for the retrospective by gathering data from the completed sprint and inviting all duties to participate, ensuring everyone understands the purpose and feels psychologically safe to share their thoughts.  

Step 2  
During the meeting, the Scrum Master uses facilitation and conflict resolution skills to guide open discussions, encourage honest feedback, and help duties identify what went well, what could be improved, and any challenges faced.  

Step 3  
The Scrum Master collaborates with all duties to capture actionable Sprint Retro Action Items and Agile Maturity Measurements, ensuring everyone takes ownership of improvements and commits to continuous growth for future sprints.
column11:
The Scrum Master duty is responsible for performing Sprint Retrospective Facilitation and is also accountable for overseeing its success. During the activity, the Scrum Master consults with a wide range of other duties—including Product Management, Product Ownership, UX Design, UX Research, UX Writing, UX Strategy, Business Analysis, Agile Coaching, Development, Account Management, Graphic Design, Product Operations, Project Management, QA Analysis, Solutions Architecture, and Information Security Analysis—to gather feedback and advice. After the activity, all duties are informed about the outcomes, including the Sprint Retro Action Items and Agile Maturity Measurements.

These duties work together by sharing insights and feedback during the retrospective, which the Scrum Master facilitates using skills like conflict resolution, meeting facilitation, and critical thinking. The Scrum Master creates a safe environment for open communication, empowering all duties to contribute honestly. column5: Storyboarding involves creating a visual sequence that tells a story or outlines a user’s journey, requiring skills like storytelling, attention to detail, critical thinking, and organization. The duty includes collaborating with others, making decisions, and managing time effectively to produce a clear and engaging storyboard deliverable. Practices such as ownership, empowerment, and psychological safety encourage confidence and creativity, which are essential for refining ideas and addressing problems logically. These skills and practices ensure the storyboard effectively communicates the user experience, helping designers anticipate user needs and improve usability, ultimately contributing to the success of the UX design process.
column6:

1. https://www.nyfa.edu/student-resources/what-is-a-storyboard-and-how-do-you-make-one/ (New York Film Academy)  
2. https://www.canva.com/learn/storyboarding/ (Canva Design School)  
3. https://www.masterclass.com/articles/how-to-create-a-storyboard (MasterClass)
column7: Storyboarding is valuable for cross-functional agile teams because it helps achieve the deliverable of a Storyboard, which is important during the Requirements milestone. Storyboarding lets a team visually plan out how a user will interact with a product or service, making it easier to spot problems and improve ideas before building anything. This activity is owned by UX (User Experience) Design and helps everyone understand what needs to be built and why, so the team can make better decisions and create a smoother experience for users.
column8: The UX Design duty works closely with other duties such as Business Analysis, Product Management, Product Ownership, UX Research, UX Strategy, and UX Writing to deliver the storyboard as part of the requirements milestone. UX Design takes ownership of organizing and visually mapping out the user experience, while Business Analysis clarifies user and business needs, and Product Management and Product Ownership ensure alignment with project goals and priorities. UX Research provides insights into user behaviors and preferences, UX Strategy guides the overall direction, and UX Writing helps craft clear, engaging narrative elements. By collaborating and sharing information, each duty contributes its expertise, ensuring the storyboard is accurate, user-focused, and ready to support the next steps in the project.
column9:
To be great at storyboarding while working with other cross-functional duties, the UX Design duty needs strong storytelling skills to create engaging and clear visual narratives, attention to detail to capture every important step in the user’s journey, and critical thinking to anticipate user needs and challenges. Collaboration and communication are essential for sharing ideas and feedback with duties like Business Analysis, Product Management, UX Research, and UX Writing. Practices such as ownership and empowerment help the UX Design duty take responsibility for the storyboard’s quality, while psychological safety encourages open discussion and creativity, making it easier to incorporate insights from all involved duties.

These skills and practices help ensure that the storyboard is accurate, user-focused, and aligned with project goals, which is crucial for delivering the requirements milestone on time. By organizing information clearly, making thoughtful decisions, and working closely with other duties, the UX Design duty can quickly address problems, adapt to feedback, and keep the project moving forward. This leads to a well-crafted storyboard that supports the next steps in the project and increases the chances of delivering successful outcomes within the set deadlines.
column10:
Here's how the UX (User Experience) Design duty carries out the activity Storyboarding with others  
Step 1  
UX Design gathers information from Business Analysis, Product Management, and Product Ownership to understand user needs, business goals, and project priorities. This helps ensure the storyboard will address the right problems and align with requirements.

Step 2  
UX Design collaborates with UX Research, UX Strategy, and UX Writing to organize ideas, map out the user journey, and create a visual sequence that clearly shows how users will interact with the product. Input from these duties helps make the storyboard accurate, user-focused, and engaging.

Step 3  
UX Design reviews the storyboard with all involved duties, encourages open feedback, and makes necessary adjustments. By taking ownership and using strong communication and decision-making skills, UX Design ensures the storyboard is clear, complete, and ready to support the next steps in the project.
column11:
For storyboarding, the UX (User Experience) Strategy duty is responsible for performing the activity by organizing and visually mapping out the user experience. The UX (User Experience) Design duty is accountable, overseeing the success and quality of the storyboard. Duties such as UX Research, UX Writing, UX Strategy, Product Ownership, Product Management, Scrum Master, Development, Graphic Design, Solutions Architecture, and Information Security Analysis are consulted, providing advice and feedback to ensure the storyboard is accurate, user-focused, and aligned with project goals. All duties, including Business Analysis, Development, Graphic Design, Information Security Analysis, Product Management, Product Operations, Product Ownership, Project Management, QA Analysis, Scrum Master, Solutions Architecture, UX Design, UX Research, UX Strategy, UX Writing, Account Management, and Agile Coaching, are informed about the work after it is completed.

These duties work together by sharing expertise and feedback throughout the storyboarding process. The responsible duty creates the storyboard, the accountable duty ensures its quality, consulted duties offer insights and suggestions, and informed duties stay updated on progress. This collaboration ensures the storyboard is well-organized, meets user and business needs, and supports the next steps in the project, resulting in an intuitive and engaging user experience.

It requires practices like decision-making, ownership, empowerment, and service leadership to ensure psychological safety and continuous growth. These skills and practices help in building influence, generating buy-in, and making well-informed decisions that align with the product vision. Effective collaboration, critical thinking, and perseverance contribute to organizing efforts and managing time efficiently, which directly impacts the success of launching and sustaining the product in the market.

column7: Strategy Definition is valuable for cross-functional agile teams because it helps to achieve deliverables like a Go-To-Market Plan and a Business Plan, which guide how a product or service will be launched and how the business will grow during milestones such as Continuous Discovery. Throughout the milestones of Continuous Discovery, the Product Management duty uses negotiation, advocacy, and strategic thinking to build consensus and generate buy-in from other duties. Attention to detail and decision-making help ensure that the plans are practical and adaptable, while continuous growth allows for ongoing improvement. These skills and practices help Product Management build influence, generate buy-in, and make informed decisions, which are crucial for delivering high-quality plans within given milestones. column5: Surveys in UX research involve carefully designing and conducting questionnaires to gather user feedback, requiring skills like attention to detail, critical thinking, and written communication to create clear and effective questions. The duty includes organizing and managing survey distribution, analyzing responses, and delivering research reports and analysis that inform design decisions. Practices such as decision-making, ownership, and psychological safety ensure that the survey process is thorough, ethical, and empowering for participants, which enhances the reliability and usefulness of the data collected. Effective time management, collaboration, and perseverance help meet project milestones like continuous discovery and acceptance testing, ultimately improving the user experience by providing actionable insights.
column6:

3. Qualtrics: Survey Methodology (https://www.qualtrics.com/experience-management/research/survey-methodology/)
column7: Name Surveys are valuable for cross-functional agile teams because they help create important deliverables like a Research Report, Research Analysis, and Research Plan, which are used during milestones such as Continuous Discovery and Acceptance Testing. This activity lets teams gather information from people to understand what users need or think, so they can make better decisions and improve their products or services.
column8: The UX Research duty works closely with Business Analysis, Product Management, Product Ownership, UX Design, and UX Strategy duties to deliver high-quality research plans, analyses, and reports throughout the project milestones. While UX Research designs and conducts surveys to gather user insights, Business Analysis helps define the right questions by clarifying business needs, and Product Management and Product Ownership ensure the research aligns with product goals and priorities. UX Design and UX Strategy duties use the findings to inform design decisions and long-term direction. By sharing information, coordinating timelines, and integrating feedback, these duties collectively ensure that the deliverables—such as the research plan, analysis, and report—are accurate, actionable, and support continuous discovery and acceptance testing milestones.
column9:
To be great at the UX Research duty when conducting surveys and working with other cross-functional duties, it is important to have strong attention to detail to create clear and unbiased questions, critical thinking and decision-making to interpret user responses accurately, and excellent written and verbal communication to explain survey goals and findings. Practices like ownership and self-organization help maintain commitment and initiative, while collaboration and psychological safety encourage open sharing of insights with Business Analysis, Product Management, Product Ownership, UX Design, and UX Strategy duties. Effective time management and perseverance ensure that surveys are designed, distributed, and analyzed thoroughly and on schedule.

These skills and practices directly affect the success of delivering research plans, analyses, and reports within project milestones. By coordinating timelines, sharing information, and integrating feedback from other duties, the UX Research duty ensures that deliverables are accurate, actionable, and aligned with business and product goals. This leads to better user experience insights, supports continuous discovery, and helps meet acceptance testing milestones, ultimately improving the quality and impact of the final product.
column10:
Here's how the UX (User Experience) Research duty carries out the activity Surveys with others  
Step 1  
The UX Research duty collaborates with Business Analysis, Product Management, and Product Ownership duties to define survey goals and create clear, unbiased questions that address both user needs and business priorities.  

Step 2  
The UX Research duty organizes and distributes the survey, ensuring effective communication and coordination with other duties to reach the right users and collect meaningful responses within the project timeline.  

Step 3  
After gathering responses, the UX Research duty analyzes the data, shares findings with UX Design and UX Strategy duties, and integrates feedback from all involved duties to produce a research report that informs design decisions and supports ongoing project milestones.
column11:
The UX Research duty is responsible for performing surveys, which means they design the questions, collect user responses, and analyze the data to understand user needs and experiences. They are also accountable, overseeing the success of the survey activity and ensuring the research is thorough and unbiased. Product Ownership, Product Management, UX Strategy, UX Design, Business Analysis, and UX Writing duties are consulted, providing advice and feedback to help shape the survey and interpret results. All duties, including those in Business Analysis, Development, Graphic Design, Information Security Analysis, Product Management, Product Operations, Product Ownership, Project Management, QA Analysis, Scrum Master, Solutions Architecture, UX Design, UX Research, UX Strategy, UX Writing, Account Management, and Agile Coaching, are informed about the survey work and its outcomes.

These duties work together by sharing information, coordinating timelines, and integrating feedback throughout the research process. The responsible and accountable UX Research duty leads the activity, while consulted duties ensure the survey aligns with business needs and product goals. Informed duties stay updated on progress and results, supporting continuous discovery and acceptance testing. This collaboration ensures that the research plan, analysis, and report are accurate, actionable, and contribute to better user experience insights and successful product outcomes.

column7: Team Role and Responsibility Definition is valuable for cross-functional agile teams because it helps to achieve deliverables like RACI Charts, which show who is Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, or Informed for different tasks, and this is important during the Intake milestone when new work or projects are being started. column8: The duty owner in product operations collaborates closely with all other duties to define and clarify who is responsible, accountable, consulted, and informed for each task by creating RACI charts. Through open communication, negotiation, and attention to detail, the duty owner ensures that every duty understands its commitments and expectations for the intake milestone. By fostering psychological safety and empowerment, the duty owner encourages others to take initiative and self-organize, which helps maintain clear organization and smooth coordination. column9:

column5: Technical Architecture Creation involves designing and documenting the structure of a system through detailed architecture diagrams, database and network architecture, entity relationship diagrams, hosting infrastructure, microservices, and system architecture specifications. This duty requires strong skills in attention to detail, critical thinking, logical thinking, problem-solving, and effective communication, both verbal and written. Practices such as decision-making, ownership, service leadership, psychological safety, and empowerment are essential to foster a collaborative and confident environment where ideas can be shared and refined. These skills and practices ensure that the architecture is well-organized, scalable, and aligned with project requirements, ultimately contributing to the success and reliability of the solution.
column6:
Here are some resources to learn about how to perform the activity:  
1. https://martinfowler.com/architecture/ (Martin Fowler’s Guide to Software Architecture)  
2. https://www.pluralsight.com/paths/software-architecture-fundamentals (Pluralsight: Software Architecture Fundamentals)  
3. https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/resources/cloud-architecture-guide/ (Microsoft Cloud Architecture Guide)
column7: Technical Architecture Creation is valuable for cross-functional agile teams because it helps achieve important deliverables like Architecture Diagrams, Database Architecture, Network Architecture, Entity Relationship Diagrams, Hosting Infrastructure, Microservices Architecture, System Architecture Diagram, and Technical Architecture Specifications, which are all needed during milestones such as Architecture and Requirements. This activity makes sure everyone understands how the different parts of a system fit together and work, so the team can build things that are reliable, organized, and easy to improve.
column8: The duty owner of Solutions Architecture collaborates closely with other duties such as Business Analysis, Development, and Product Ownership to deliver the technical architecture deliverables required at each project milestone. The Solutions Architecture duty relies on input from Business Analysis to understand requirements and constraints, works with Development to ensure the proposed architecture is feasible and scalable, and coordinates with Product Ownership to align technical decisions with product goals. Through effective communication, decision-making, and shared ownership, these duties collectively refine and validate architecture diagrams, database structures, network layouts, and technical specifications, ensuring that each deliverable meets the needs of the project and supports successful milestone completion.
column9:
To be great at Technical Architecture Creation, the Solutions Architecture duty needs strong attention to detail, critical and logical thinking, and effective problem-solving to design clear and robust architecture diagrams, database structures, and network layouts. Collaboration and communication skills are essential for working with Business Analysis, Development, and Product Ownership duties, ensuring that requirements are understood and technical decisions are aligned with product goals. Practices like decision-making, ownership, service leadership, psychological safety, and empowerment help create an environment where ideas can be shared openly, responsibilities are clear, and solutions are refined through collective input.

These skills and practices directly affect the ability to deliver high-quality technical architecture deliverables within project milestones. Clear communication and shared ownership help prevent misunderstandings and rework, while strong organization and time management ensure that architecture diagrams and specifications are completed on schedule. By fostering psychological safety and empowerment, the duty can confidently make decisions and adapt to changes, supporting the successful completion of each milestone and ensuring the technical foundation is solid and scalable.
column10:
Here's how the Solutions Architecture duty carries out the activity Technical Architecture Creation with others  
Step 1  
The Solutions Architecture duty gathers input from the Business Analysis duty to understand project requirements, constraints, and business goals. This information is used to start designing architecture diagrams, database structures, and network layouts that will support the solution.

Step 2  
The Solutions Architecture duty collaborates with the Development duty to review and refine the proposed technical architecture, ensuring that it is feasible, scalable, and aligns with technical best practices. Feedback is incorporated to improve clarity and precision in the documentation.

Step 3  
The Solutions Architecture duty coordinates with the Product Ownership duty to make sure that all technical decisions and deliverables, such as architecture diagrams and technical specifications, align with the overall product goals and project milestones. Throughout the process, effective communication, decision-making, and shared ownership help ensure that the technical architecture is robust, well-documented, and ready for implementation.
column11:
For Technical Architecture Creation, the duty of Solutions Architecture is responsible for designing the architecture diagrams, database structures, and network layouts. Solutions Architecture is also accountable, overseeing the success and quality of the technical architecture deliverables. Duties such as UX (User Experience) Design, UX (User Experience) Research, UX (User Experience) Writing, UX (User Experience) Strategy, Business Analysis, Product Management, and Scrum Master are consulted, providing advice and feedback to ensure the architecture meets user needs, business requirements, and project constraints. All duties, including Business Analysis, Development, Graphic Design, Information Security Analysis, Product Management, Product Operations, Product Ownership, Project Management, QA Analysis, Scrum Master, Solutions Architecture, UX (User Experience) Design, UX (User Experience) Research, UX (User Experience) Strategy, UX (User Experience) Writing, Account Management, and Agile Coaching, are informed about the work after it is completed.

These duties work together by sharing information, giving feedback, and making decisions that shape the technical architecture. Solutions Architecture leads the activity, gathers input from consulted duties to refine and validate the architecture, and ensures everyone is kept up to date. This collaboration, supported by skills like critical thinking, communication, and decision-making, ensures the technical architecture is robust, scalable, and aligned with project goals, leading to successful project milestones.
